ground_truth.title: Weight Loss Medication Guide: Types, Costs and How They Work
ground_truth.author: Kate Bayless
ground_truth.main_content: America has a weight problem. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 40% of adults in the United States are obese, with nearly 1 in 10 listed as severely obese. In recent years, weight loss medications have become popular as valuable tools to complement a healthy diet and regular exercise. Prescription weight loss medications are designed to help manage body weight by blocking fat absorption, curbing appetite or increasing feelings of fullness to reduce food consumption. The national average cost of weight loss medications can vary between $55 per bottle for Alli and $3,670 per 10 milligrams of Imcivree. Coverage of weight loss medications by health insurance providers varies greatly. Medical insurance usually only covers procedures and medications that are deemed medically necessary for a specific condition. Depending on the type you use, weight loss medications work in the body in three main ways: decrease hunger and craving, increase satiety or feelings of fullness, and decrease your body's ability to absorb fat from food. Weight loss medications like Mounjaro and Ozempic are prescription medications and, therefore, not available over the counter. A variety of medications have been approved by the FDA as safe for aiding weight loss. When paired with a healthy diet and regular exercise, many people can see a total weight loss of 3% to 12% with some medications. Gastrointestinal issues are the most common side effect of most weight loss medications. Alternatives to weight loss medications include weight loss surgery, weight loss programs, liposuction, and non-invasive fat removal treatments.
